text: Woke up convinced it was Monday. It was Saturday. I wrote the day in big letters on a sticky note to calm down.
Church in the morning. I kept checking my purse for my keys every five minutes.
Sorted the mail into two piles: 'pay' and 'later'. The 'later' pile is winning.
I hate how quickly I get flustered when something changes. My brain feels slower, like it needs a few seconds to load.
Walked around the block and tried to name the trees like I used to. Got half of them right, I think.
Made a 'landing zone' by the door: a tray for mail and a hook for keys. It looks tidy. I hope I can keep it that way.
Melissa asked if I’m sleeping okay. I said yes, but truth is I wake up at 3am and replay conversations.
Grocery store felt loud today. I left without the one thing I went for (tea). Came home with crackers instead.
I keep saying 'I’m fine' because it’s easier than explaining. But I’m not fine all the time.
Wrote tomorrow’s plan on an index card: 'calendar, meds, breakfast, call Melissa'. Small steps.
Printed the senior center activities email and stuck it on the fridge. Bridge is circled.
I practiced what to say to the doctor: 'I forget appointments and misplace things.' Saying it out loud made it real.
Dr. Patel was kind. I brought my medication list like a good student and still felt like I was failing a test.
Picked up prescriptions and a pill organizer. The pharmacist explained it twice. I nodded like I understood.
Melissa emailed a 'weekly plan' and it helped. I hate needing it, but I also feel lighter.
Bridge group. I blanked on two names and covered by talking about the weather. No one seemed to notice.
Tried the 'one calendar' rule today. Put the paper planner in a drawer and left the fridge calendar visible.
Got a bill email and felt my stomach drop. I used to be organized. I miss that version of me.
Caregiver check-in with Melissa. We made a short list: meds, bills, appointments. Lists feel like rails.
Filled out memory clinic forms slowly, one page at a time. I kept rereading the same questions.
Memory clinic intake was exhausting. I wanted to prove I’m capable, but the tests don’t care about pride.
They said to bring hearing aids and glasses next time. I laughed because I can’t even keep track of my glasses.
Received a scary bank email about 'unusual activity.' I didn’t click. I put it in a folder called 'suspicious.'
Bank call to set up AutoPay. I asked them to keep it simple. I wrote the steps down immediately.
Put a phone scam script by the landline. It makes me feel a little braver.
Water aerobics was fun and confusing. I forgot where the locker room was and followed someone confidently.
Bridge again. I remembered to bring snacks. Small win.
I keep rehearsing tomorrow’s dentist appointment in my head like a song so I won’t miss it.
Dental cleaning done. I asked them to write the next date on a card and email it too.
Home safety visit. They pointed out rugs I’ve stepped over for 20 years. I agreed anyway.
Lunch with June felt normal. I stayed present by focusing on her earrings and her laugh.
Spent the morning labeling drawers. It’s weirdly comforting, like making the house speak clearly.
OT session: kitchen routines. The therapist said 'systems beat memory.' I wrote that down.
I keep putting my glasses in nonsense places. The therapist suggested a tray and a rule: only two places.
Neurology consult. They used the phrase 'mild cognitive impairment' and then 'early Alzheimer's.' The room got very quiet.
I stared at the diagnosis handout for ten minutes. Then I folded it neatly like that would make it smaller.
Made a simple weekly sheet: appointments, pills, bills. Put it in a clear sleeve on the fridge.
Bridge group. I brought my little name card in my wallet and it helped more than I expected.
I keep imagining my brain like a library where some books are mis-shelved. Not gone—just harder to find.
Water aerobics. I remembered the locker room today without hesitation. That felt like sunlight.
First memory support group. Hearing other people describe the same shame made mine loosen a bit.
Mother’s Day shopping with Melissa. The mall was a maze but she parked near the food court like she promised.
Bloodwork. I set two reminders and still arrived ten minutes early, just to be safe.
Sometimes I can’t tell if I’m tired or 'foggy.' I’m learning to treat fog like weather: it passes.
Video call with Ben. I forgot what I was saying mid-sentence and he waited calmly. That kindness mattered.
Cleaned out a kitchen drawer and found three identical notepads. Apparently I kept buying them because I forgot I had them.
Picked up the new memory medication. I practiced saying the name in the car so I wouldn’t feel silly at the counter.
The pharmacist’s email said 'do not double-dose.' That line sits heavy. I’m building guardrails.
Got approved for the senior shuttle. I felt both relieved and offended at the same time.
Water aerobics. I chatted with a woman named Linda and immediately forgot her name. Wrote it down in the car.
Bridge group. The room smelled like coffee and old playing cards. Comforting.
Farmers market. Bought strawberries and then couldn’t find my way back to the car for a minute. Breathed through it.
Hearing test. The audiologist spoke slowly and it helped. I didn’t realize how much I rely on context clues.
Senior shuttle orientation. So many rules. I highlighted the phone number in neon yellow.
I’m learning to ask for repeats without apologizing. Apologies don’t fix memory.
OT session: phone reminders. We practiced 'one Today screen.' I felt like a beginner again.
Printed an emergency contact list and taped it by the phone. It looks dramatic but also calming.
Grandkids visit. They don’t mind repeating themselves. They just want snacks and attention.
After everyone left, the house felt too quiet. I replayed their voices to keep the quiet away.
Tested the shuttle by booking one ride. I practiced reading my address out loud, slowly.
Sometimes I forget the middle of a story but remember exactly how it felt. Feelings stick.
Pool day. I packed twice and still forgot my towel. Borrowed one and tried not to spiral.
Fourth of July picnic. I sat in the shade like Melissa insisted. I kept counting the kids to feel grounded.
Fireworks were loud. My brain felt even louder. I went to bed early without guilt.
Eye exam. I admitted I keep losing glasses. They suggested a chain. I said yes.
Bridge group. I won one hand and forgot immediately how I did it.
Put a sign inside the fridge door: 'NO KEYS / NO GLASSES.' It made me laugh and also it worked once.
Support group topic was 'grief without catastrophe.' That phrase stayed with me.
Front step railing fixed. I realized I’ve been holding my breath on the stairs. Now I exhale.
Made a small 'go bag' for appointments: ID, cards, a snack, the medication list. Less scrambling.
Practiced my balance exercises by the counter. I talked to myself like a coach.
PT for balance. The therapist said, 'strong legs = more independence.' I like that math.
I’m better at asking for help than I was in March. Not good, but better.
Grocery run. I followed my list exactly. It felt like a tiny superpower.
Water aerobics. The instructor remembered my name. I almost cried.
I still get embarrassed when I repeat myself. But repeating is also how I learn now.
Support group. I shared my 'landing zone' trick. Felt good to contribute, not just receive.
Medication review with pharmacist. We made a breakfast routine and two reminders. I taped the plan near the kettle.
The pillbox photo on my phone is oddly reassuring. Proof that I set things up correctly.
Bridge group. Time change threw me off but I still got there. I’m proud of that.
Changed the fire alarm batteries. I set a timer and did it right away so I wouldn’t forget.
Past-due notice scared me. I’m doing my best, but the paper feels like it multiplies overnight.
Messaged the bank to confirm AutoPay. I kept the email short and direct.
Went through the Bills folder with Melissa on speakerphone. We checked off items like a game.
Some days I feel almost normal. Then I forget a word and the floor drops again.
Water aerobics. I wrote 'POOL TOWEL' in huge letters on my list and it worked.
Bridge group. I used a cover phrase when I forgot someone’s husband’s name: 'How have you both been?'
I’m nervous about the neurology follow-up. I don’t want worse news, but I also want clarity.
Neurology follow-up. Medication tweak. Melissa took notes while I watched her pen like it was a lifeline.
Made a little prep sheet for the lawyer meeting: what I want, what scares me, what I won’t give up.
Estate planning meeting was hard but not as grim as I expected. It felt like choosing my future, not losing it.
Support group. Someone said, 'Ask for help early, not after the fall.' I wrote it down.
Lunch after church. I told a friend, quietly, that I’m having memory trouble. She squeezed my hand and changed the subject gently.
I keep thinking about driving. I’m still okay on familiar roads, but I’m building backup options so it’s not a cliff.
PT fall prevention. I practiced standing on one foot by the counter like a flamingo.
Made a 'quiet kit' for outings: water, tissues, a small snack, and my contact list.
I’m noticing patterns: mornings are clearer, afternoons fuzzier. I plan important things early now.
Bridge group. I laughed more than I played. That counts.
Water aerobics. Someone asked if I wanted to carpool and I said no, then regretted it. Pride is stubborn.
I’m trying to treat 'help' like a tool, not a verdict. Tools are normal.
Flu shot. I brought my record and asked them to write it down right away.
Fall festival. I took breaks in the quiet room like the flyer suggested. That was smart.
Support group. We traded reminder hacks. Mine: two alarms, one 'Are you sure?' alarm.
I forgot a word for 'umbrella' and called it the 'rain stick.' We laughed. I’m collecting these moments.
Dropped the car at Maple Auto. I took a photo of where I parked because I’ve wandered lots before.
Put my appointment bag by the door for tomorrow. I’m learning to do things 'too early' on purpose.
Halloween used to be costumes and chaos. Now it’s a quiet porch light and a cup of tea. Still okay.
Water aerobics. I felt strong today. Strength is a kind of memory too.
Bridge group. I forgot my own score and shrugged. No one died.
Prepared for the MRI: no jewelry, comfy clothes, paperwork in one envelope. The checklist helped.
MRI day. I focused on breathing and counting. I didn’t bolt. That felt like winning.
Wrote down three questions for the doctor so I won’t forget them at the next visit.
Support group topic was routines. I shared my kettle checklist and someone asked for a copy.
Book club at the library. I didn’t finish the book but I still went and listened. That counts too.
Thanksgiving makes me nervous—so many moving parts. Melissa keeps saying, 'Just show up.' I’ll try.
Thanksgiving lunch. I forgot a grandchild’s teacher’s name and no one cared. The pie was excellent.
I’m tired after big gatherings. The next day feels like a hangover, but from words.
Water aerobics. The pool is quieter in winter. I like the quiet.
Bridge group. Someone brought peppermint candies and I pocketed one like a talisman.
I keep losing track of what I’ve already told people. It’s like living with a skip in the record.
Holiday cards: I made an address list instead of trusting my brain. It feels practical, not sad.
Clinic check-in call (rescheduled). I put the phone on speaker and took notes slowly.
Support group. We practiced forgiving ourselves. I’m not great at it yet.
Choir rehearsal. I lost my place in the binder and someone pointed gently. I stayed anyway.
Wrapped small gifts with a timer so I wouldn’t get stuck perfectionizing. The timer works on me.
Pharmacy refill reminder came in. I replied right away because if I wait, it vanishes from my mind.
Christmas Eve service. Candlelight made everything soft. I felt grateful for softness.
Reorganized the 'Bills' folder into months. It’s not elegant, but it’s visible.
New Year’s used to mean resolutions. Now it means: keep my world stable and kind.
Water aerobics. I made a friend again and wrote her name (Linda) on my hand like a teenager.
Bridge group. I brought my name cheat sheet and didn’t need it. That felt good.
Some mornings I forget I have a diagnosis. Then I misplace something and remember sharply.
Annual physical. I handed over my updated medication list like a professional. The nurse thanked me.
I’m learning the difference between independence and isolation. Independence can include support.
Support group. We talked about winter blues. I committed to one social thing per week.
Tax documents day. I used a timer and a three-pile method. Stopped before I got overwhelmed.
I found my SSA form in the freezer. Not funny in the moment, but funny now.
Picked up pharmacy refill. I checked it off on my fridge sheet the second I got home.
I’m less afraid of paperwork when I treat it like a short session, not a marathon.
Water aerobics. I forgot my locker number and asked without shame. Shame wastes time.
Bridge group. I told one person, casually, 'I mix up names sometimes.' It was freeing.
Neurology follow-up this week. I’m bringing the symptom log so my words don’t have to carry everything.
Neurology follow-up went smoothly. Melissa’s notes filled in the gaps. I’m grateful for her steadiness.
Valentine’s brunch with June. We talked about normal things. I needed that more than I knew.
Support group. I brought cookies. People smiled. I like being useful.
Sometimes I worry my world is shrinking. Then I look at my calendar and realize I’m still living.
PT balance reassessment. I can stand longer on one foot now. Improvement is real, even now.
Grocery delivery arrived. I followed the checklist: cold things first, then pantry. It felt orderly.
I’m grateful for checklists. They don’t judge me. They just wait patiently.
If someone asked what I’ve learned this year: slow down, write it down, and let love help.
